stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 at 39 weeks of gestation collapsed in second stage of labour with sudden dyspnoea, cyanosis and hypotension. She received CPR for 4 minutes with return of spontaneous circulation. She is now intubated and ventilated on an adrenaline infusion. Massive vaginal bleeding has started since collapse with no clot formation visible. The team has activated the obstetric emergency code.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 80/40 on adrenaline 0.1 mcg/kg/min, HR 130, RR 18 ventilated, SpO2 88% on FiO2 1.0, temp 36.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Generalised oozing from intravenous sites and gums; ongoing brisk bleeding per vagina with no clot; uterus boggy; fetal heart rate 90 with prolonged deceleration
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78, platelets 60, fibrinogen 0.8, PT 30 seconds, APTT 65 seconds, lactate 8.4, pH 7.18; cross-match in progress

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate priority while continuing resuscitation?

options.text: Massive transfusion protocol with balanced blood products, expedite delivery and intensive care
options.rationale: Amniotic fluid embolism causes cardiopulmonary collapse with DIC; treatment is supportive resuscitation with balanced blood products via the massive transfusion protocol, immediate delivery to relieve aortocaval compression and assist resuscitation, cryoprecipitate for hypofibrinogenaemia, and intensive care support. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management: Critical bleeding and massive transfusion)
source_refs: NBA Patient Blood Management: Critical bleeding and massive transfusion

options.text: IV unfractionated heparin 5000 unit bolus then continuous infusion targeting APTT 60-80 seconds
options.rationale: Heparin is contraindicated in active haemorrhage with DIC; AFE coagulopathy is treated with product replacement rather than systemic anticoagulation. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management: Critical bleeding and massive transfusion)
source_refs: NBA Patient Blood Management: Critical bleeding and massive transfusion

options.text: IV tranexamic acid 1 g and IM ergometrine 250 micrograms given postpartum haemorrhage features
options.rationale: TXA and uterotonics treat uterine atony PPH but are inadequate for AFE with DIC; balanced blood product resuscitation and delivery are the primary actions. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse statement

options.text: Defer delivery until coagulation parameters are corrected then proceed to caesarean section here
options.rationale: Delaying delivery worsens maternal resuscitation efforts and fetal outcome; expedited delivery is part of the resuscitation in AFE with maternal collapse. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse statement

options.text: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days targeting the anaphylactoid component of embolism
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in AFE and do not replace the priorities of resuscitation, product replacement and delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse statement

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism causes sudden cardiopulmonary collapse with DIC during labour or delivery. Management is supportive resuscitation with airway protection and vasopressors, massive transfusion protocol with balanced blood products and cryoprecipitate, expedited delivery to assist maternal resuscitation, and intensive care support. Heparin is contraindicated during active haemorrhage with DIC.
key_points: AFE requires massive transfusion protocol with balanced blood products
Expedited delivery is part of maternal resuscitation in AFE collapse
Heparin is contraindicated; product replacement treats DIC
explanation.pearls: Cryoprecipitate and fibrinogen concentrate target fibrinogen above 2 g/L in obstetric haemorrhage; consider early activation of the obstetric massive transfusion pathway with named haematology cover.

sources.label: NBA Patient Blood Management: Critical bleeding and massive transfusion

stem: A 58-year-old man presents to a regional emergency department with 40 minutes of crushing central chest pain radiating to the left jaw. He is a current smoker and has hypertension. He has had no previous cardiac events. The nearest 24-hour cardiac catheterisation laboratory is reached by helicopter within 90 minutes, with an expected total door-to-balloon time of 110 minutes from now. There are no contraindications to fibrinolysis.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 140/85, HR 95 sinus, RR 20, SpO2 96% on 2 L oxygen via nasal prongs, temp 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, in pain; chest clear; heart sounds normal with no murmur; peripheries cool
data_table.Investigations: ECG: ST elevation 3 mm V1 to V4 with reciprocal inferior depression; troponin pending; potassium 4.0; creatinine 80

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate reperfusion strategy?

options.text: Weight-based intravenous tenecteplase bolus immediately at the current site of presentation
options.rationale: Fibrinolysis is the alternative when primary PCI is not achievable within 120 minutes; with a door-to-balloon under 120 minutes PCI is preferred. (Source: National Heart Foundation of Australia: STEMI clinical care standard)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation of Australia: STEMI clinical care standard

options.text: Transfer immediately for primary PCI given expected door-to-balloon under 120 minutes
options.rationale: Australian Heart Foundation guidance recommends primary PCI when first medical contact to device time is under 120 minutes; the expected 110 minutes door-to-balloon makes transfer the preferred reperfusion strategy. (Source: National Heart Foundation of Australia: STEMI clinical care standard)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation of Australia: STEMI clinical care standard

options.text: Loading dose aspirin and ticagrelor with conservative management given short pain duration
options.rationale: Conservative management does not provide reperfusion in STEMI; antiplatelets are adjuncts but the patient still needs primary PCI or fibrinolysis. (Source: eTG Cardiovascular: Acute coronary syndromes)
source_refs: eTG Cardiovascular: Acute coronary syndromes

options.text: IV unfractionated heparin alone while organising cath lab transfer the following morning
options.rationale: Delaying reperfusion to the next day in an active STEMI is not acceptable; reperfusion must be expedited within minutes to hours. (Source: National Heart Foundation of Australia: STEMI clinical care standard)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation of Australia: STEMI clinical care standard

options.text: Hospital admission for serial troponins and ECG monitoring before any reperfusion decision
options.rationale: ST elevation on ECG with typical pain establishes STEMI and mandates immediate reperfusion; waiting for troponin would delay therapy and increase infarct size. (Source: National Heart Foundation of Australia: STEMI clinical care standard)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation of Australia: STEMI clinical care standard

explanation.summary: Primary PCI is the preferred reperfusion strategy in STEMI when first medical contact to device time can be achieved within 120 minutes; otherwise fibrinolysis is given followed by transfer for angiography. Aspirin and a P2Y12 inhibitor are adjuncts but do not replace reperfusion. Waiting for troponins delays time-critical therapy.
key_points: Primary PCI is preferred when door-to-balloon is within 120 minutes
Fibrinolysis is used when timely PCI is not achievable
ECG ST elevation with typical pain establishes STEMI and mandates reperfusion
explanation.pearls: Pre-treatment includes aspirin 300 mg loading, a P2Y12 inhibitor such as ticagrelor 180 mg, and parenteral anticoagulation; transfer should not be delayed for these.

sources.label: National Heart Foundation of Australia: STEMI clinical care standard

stem: A 72-year-old man with severe COPD on triple inhaler therapy presents to the emergency department with 3 days of increasing breathlessness and productive purulent sputum. He uses 1 L of home oxygen overnight. He has been a 60 pack-year smoker who recently quit. He is currently sitting upright and able to speak in short sentences.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 32, SpO2 84% in room air, HR 110, BP 145/85, temp 37.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Tripoding, accessory muscle use, audible expiratory wheeze; chest hyperinflated with bilateral wheeze; mild ankle oedema; GCS 14
data_table.Investigations: ABG in room air: pH 7.28, pCO2 78 mmHg, pO2 52 mmHg, HCO3 28; chest x-ray: hyperinflation, no consolidation

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intubate and mechanically ventilate immediately given the degree of respiratory acidosis on ABG
options.rationale: Invasive ventilation is reserved for NIV failure, severely impaired conscious state or other contraindications to NIV; this patient meets criteria for NIV first. (Source: COPD-X Plan: Australian and New Zealand guidelines for COPD)
source_refs: COPD-X Plan: Australian and New Zealand guidelines for COPD

options.text: High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask targeting SpO2 above 95 with bronchodilators and steroids
options.rationale: Uncontrolled high-flow oxygen risks worsening hypercapnia in COPD; controlled oxygen via Venturi titrated to SpO2 88-92% is recommended. (Source: COPD-X Plan: Australian and New Zealand guidelines for COPD)
source_refs: COPD-X Plan: Australian and New Zealand guidelines for COPD

options.text: Controlled oxygen 88-92%, BiPAP NIV, nebulised bronchodilators, oral prednisolone and antibiotics
options.rationale: Hypercapnic AECOPD with pH below 7.35 is treated with controlled oxygen titrated to SpO2 88-92%, BiPAP non-invasive ventilation, nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium, oral prednisolone 30-50 mg and antibiotic if purulent sputum or consolidation. (Source: COPD-X Plan: Australian and New Zealand guidelines for COPD)
source_refs: COPD-X Plan: Australian and New Zealand guidelines for COPD

options.text: Nebulised magnesium sulfate 2 g and IV aminophylline infusion as bronchodilator escalation therapy
options.rationale: Magnesium and aminophylline are not first-line in AECOPD and add toxicity; standard nebulised bronchodilators with NIV and oxygen control are first-line. (Source: eTG Respiratory: Acute exacerbation of COPD)
source_refs: eTG Respiratory: Acute exacerbation of COPD

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 200 mg with empiric antibiotics pending sputum culture and sensitivity results
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone is as effective as IV hydrocortisone in AECOPD and is preferred; corticosteroids alone do not address hypercapnic failure. (Source: eTG Respiratory: Acute exacerbation of COPD)
source_refs: eTG Respiratory: Acute exacerbation of COPD

explanation.summary: Hypercapnic AECOPD with pH below 7.35 is managed with controlled oxygen targeting SpO2 88-92%, BiPAP non-invasive ventilation, nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium, oral prednisolone 30-50 mg for 5 days, and antibiotics if sputum is purulent. Invasive ventilation is reserved for NIV failure or severe impaired conscious state.
key_points: Target SpO2 88-92% in COPD to avoid worsening hypercapnia
BiPAP reduces intubation in pH below 7.35 hypercapnic AECOPD
Oral prednisolone 30-50 mg for 5 days is as effective as IV steroid
explanation.pearls: Antibiotic choice in AECOPD is doxycycline or amoxicillin in Australia; targeted therapy for Pseudomonas is reserved for severe disease, recent hospitalisation or known colonisation.

sources.label: COPD-X Plan: Australian and New Zealand guidelines for COPD
eTG Respiratory: Acute exacerbation of COPD

subtopic_detail: Add SGLT2i in T2DM with established ASCVD
stem: A 62-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 5 years attends his GP for review. His HbA1c is 8.2 percent, up from 7.5 percent 3 months ago, on metformin extended release 2 g daily. He had a percutaneous coronary intervention to the LAD 2 years ago and is on aspirin, atorvastatin, perindopril and metoprolol. He has no symptoms of hypoglycaemia and his renal function is preserved. His BMI is 31.

data_table.Examination: Cardiovascular and respiratory examination normal; lower limbs no oedema, foot pulses present, monofilament intact bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 8.2%, eGFR 65, ACR 4, fasting glucose 9.2, LDL 1.8, K 4.1, sodium 138; ECG sinus rhythm

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate add-on glucose-lowering therapy?

options.text: Add gliclazide modified release 60 mg daily for additional glycaemic control today
options.rationale: Sulfonylureas do not provide cardiovascular benefit and risk hypoglycaemia; an SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 receptor agonist is preferred in established ASCVD. (Source: Australian Diabetes Society: T2DM management algorithm)
source_refs: Australian Diabetes Society: T2DM management algorithm

options.text: Add basal insulin glargine 10 units at night titrated to fasting glucose 4-7 mmol/L
options.rationale: Basal insulin is escalation for refractory hyperglycaemia or symptomatic hyperglycaemia; in established ASCVD an SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 receptor agonist is preferred first. (Source: RACGP Handbook: Management of T2 diabetes)
source_refs: RACGP Handbook: Management of T2 diabetes

options.text: Switch metformin to a DPP-4 inhibitor sitagliptin 100 mg daily for ease of administration
options.rationale: DPP-4 inhibitors are glucose-lowering only and lack cardiovascular and renal protective effects; metformin should be retained as background therapy. (Source: Australian Diabetes Society: T2DM management algorithm)
source_refs: Australian Diabetes Society: T2DM management algorithm

options.text: Add SGLT2 inhibitor empagliflozin 10 mg daily for cardiovascular and renal benefit
options.rationale: Australian Diabetes Society guidance recommends adding an SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 receptor agonist in T2DM with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease regardless of HbA1c, due to reductions in MACE, heart failure and renal events. (Source: Australian Diabetes Society: T2DM management algorithm)
source_refs: Australian Diabetes Society: T2DM management algorithm

options.text: Add thiazolidinedione pioglitazone 30 mg daily for its weight-neutral glycaemic profile
options.rationale: Pioglitazone increases heart failure risk and weight gain and is not preferred in established ischaemic heart disease in current Australian guidance. (Source: RACGP Handbook: Management of T2 diabetes)
source_refs: RACGP Handbook: Management of T2 diabetes

explanation.summary: In T2DM with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, an SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 receptor agonist is added regardless of HbA1c due to proven cardiovascular and renal benefits. Sulfonylureas and DPP-4 inhibitors do not provide cardiovascular protection. Basal insulin is reserved for refractory hyperglycaemia.
key_points: Add SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA to metformin in T2DM with established ASCVD
Sulfonylureas and DPP-4 inhibitors lack cardiovascular benefit
Pioglitazone is avoided in known heart failure or high HF risk
explanation.pearls: Counsel patients commencing SGLT2 inhibitors about sick-day rules (withhold during acute illness or dehydration), genital mycotic infection risk and the rare risk of euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis.

sources.label: Australian Diabetes Society: T2DM management algorithm
RACGP Handbook: Management of T2 diabetes

stem: A 68-year-old woman attends her GP with 4 months of fatigue, cold intolerance, a 4 kg weight gain and constipation. She has a history of stable ischaemic heart disease (PCI 6 years ago) on aspirin, metoprolol and atorvastatin. She is otherwise well and not on any antithyroid medication. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Diffuse goitre; dry skin; bradycardia regular; reflexes slow to relax; no peripheral oedema; ECG sinus rhythm with no ischaemic change
data_table.Investigations: TSH 25 mIU/L (0.4-4.0), free T4 8 pmol/L (10-22), TPO antibodies positive at 350; HbA1c 5.5, LFTs normal, troponin negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initiation of thyroid hormone replacement?

options.text: Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg daily (approximately 100 mcg) as a full replacement dose now
options.rationale: Full weight-based replacement in older adults with IHD risks precipitating angina or arrhythmia; low-dose initiation with up-titration is recommended. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Hypothyroidism initiation guide)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Hypothyroidism initiation guide

options.text: Combined levothyroxine 50 mcg with liothyronine 10 mcg daily for symptom control
options.rationale: Combination therapy is not recommended initially and has no proven advantage over levothyroxine monotherapy in Australian guidance. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Hypothyroidism initiation guide)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Hypothyroidism initiation guide

options.text: Defer treatment and recheck TSH and free T4 in 6 weeks before commencing therapy
options.rationale: Symptomatic overt primary hypothyroidism with TSH 25 and low free T4 warrants prompt initiation, not deferral. (Source: AMH: Levothyroxine)
source_refs: AMH: Levothyroxine

options.text: Carbimazole 5 mg daily given the autoimmune thyroid disease features and positive TPO
options.rationale: Carbimazole is an antithyroid drug for hyperthyroidism; it is contraindicated in this patient with primary hypothyroidism. (Source: AMH: Levothyroxine)
source_refs: AMH: Levothyroxine

options.text: Levothyroxine 25 mcg daily for 4 weeks then up-titrate guided by TSH at 6 weekly intervals
options.rationale: Older adults with ischaemic heart disease start levothyroxine at 25 mcg daily and up-titrate every 4 to 6 weeks to avoid precipitating angina or arrhythmia; aim for TSH in the lower half of the normal range. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Hypothyroidism initiation guide)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Hypothyroidism initiation guide

explanation.summary: Initiation of levothyroxine in older adults with ischaemic heart disease is at a low dose (25 mcg daily) with up-titration every 4 to 6 weeks to avoid precipitating myocardial ischaemia or arrhythmia. Full weight-based replacement is reserved for young, fit adults. TSH is rechecked 6-8 weeks after each dose change.
key_points: Start levothyroxine at 25 mcg/day in older adults with IHD
Up-titrate every 4-6 weeks guided by TSH
Avoid combination T4/T3 as initial therapy in Australian guidance
explanation.pearls: If overt hypothyroidism is severe or there is myxoedema coma, intravenous levothyroxine and hydrocortisone are used and ICU support arranged; this differs from outpatient initiation.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia: Hypothyroidism initiation guide
